title: Numbers & Time
shortSummary: Master numbers 0-100 and learn to tell the time, ask for prices, and handle dates in Spanish.
culturalNote: Time is treated more flexibly in many Spanish-speaking cultures. Being 15-30 minutes late to social events is normal and expected — it's called 'la hora latina'. However, punctuality is valued in professional settings, especially in Spain. When giving prices, many Latin American countries use the dollar sign but say 'pesos' or 'soles'.

keyPhrases.context: The standard way to ask the time
For all hours except 1:00 (which uses 'Es la una')

keyPhrases.spanish: Son las ocho de la mañana
keyPhrases.pronunciation: sohn lahs OH-choh deh lah mah-NYAH-nah
keyPhrases.context: Adding time of day for clarity
Literally 'how many years do you have?'
In Spanish you 'have' years, not 'are' years
Days of the week are not capitalised in Spanish

grammar.explanation: Use 'es la' for 1:00 (singular) and 'son las' for all other hours (plural). Add 'y' (and) for minutes past, and 'menos' (minus) for minutes before the hour.
grammar.commonMistake: Saying 'son la una' instead of 'es la una' — 1:00 is singular, so use 'es'.

grammar.explanation: Unlike English where you 'are' a certain age, Spanish says you 'have' years. This is one of the most common mistakes for English speakers.

examples.en: She is 20 years old (she has 20 years)

grammar.commonMistake: Saying 'soy treinta años' (I am 30 years) instead of 'tengo treinta años' (I have 30 years).

grammar.rule: Numbers 1-30 are single words; 31+ are compound
grammar.explanation: Numbers 1-15 each have unique forms. 16-29 are written as one word (dieciséis, veintidós). From 31 onward, they separate: treinta y uno, cuarenta y dos, etc.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing 'veinte y uno' as separate words instead of the single word 'veintiuno'.

dialogue.setup: Ana is at a market stall buying fruit. The vendor, Don Pedro, helps her.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.english: Good morning. How much are the apples?

lines.speaker: Don Pedro
lines.spanish: Buenos días. Dos euros el kilo.
lines.english: Good morning. Two euros per kilo.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.spanish: Bien, quiero tres kilos, por favor.
lines.english: Good, I'd like three kilos, please.

lines.speaker: Don Pedro
lines.english: Your change: four euros. Thank you!

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.english: Thanks. What time do you close?

lines.speaker: Don Pedro
lines.spanish: Cerramos a las dos de la tarde.
lines.english: We close at two in the afternoon.

quiz.question: How do you say 'What time is it?' in Spanish?
quiz.explanation: Literally 'What hour is it?' — this is the standard way to ask the time.

quiz.question: _____ las tres y media. (It's 3:30)
quiz.explanation: Use 'son' for all hours except 1:00 (which uses 'es la una').

quiz.question: How do you say 'I'm 20 years old' in Spanish?
quiz.explanation: In Spanish you 'have' years (tener) — you don't 'are' years.

quiz.question: How do you say the number 17 in Spanish?
quiz.explanation: Numbers 16-19 combine 'dieci-' (ten and) with the unit: diecisiete = diez y siete.

quiz.question: Arrange: euros / cuesta / cinco
quiz.explanation: The verb 'cuesta' (it costs) comes first, then the amount.
